chunks:
# {module}_intent.yaml
# Intent Artifact Template for Intent-Verified Development (IVD)
# 
# IVD: THE FRAMEWORK FOR THE AI AGENTS ERA
# =========================================
# The AI writes this intent artifact, not the human.
# The AI implements against this intent (code, docs, architecture, research, etc.).
# The AI verifies its output against the constraints.
# Hallucinations are caught. Turns drop to one.
#
# SCOPE: Any AI-produced artifact (code, architecture, docs, research, books, processes)
#
# WORKFLOW:
#   1. Human describes what they want (natural language)
#   2. AI writes this structured intent artifact
#   3. Human reviews: "Is this what I meant?"
#   4. AI implements against the intent
#   5. AI verifies: "Does my output pass the constraints?"
#
# NAMING PATTERNS (required for tool discovery):
#   System:   {project}_system_intent.yaml   (e.g., system_intent.yaml, book_system_intent.yaml)
#   Workflow: {workflow}_intent.yaml         (e.g., lead_qualification_intent.yaml)
#   Module:   {module}_intent.yaml           (e.g., reviewer_intent.yaml) - REQUIRED pattern for tools
#   Variant:  {variant}_{module}_intent.yaml (e.g., erik_reviewer_intent.yaml)
#   Task:     {function}_intent.yaml         (e.g., review_article_intent.yaml)
#
# PLACEMENT (required for tool discovery):
#   System:   /system_intent.yaml or /{project}_system_intent.yaml (repo root)
#   Workflow: /workflows/{name}_intent.yaml  OR  {coordinator_dir}/{name}_intent.yaml (when single-orchestrator; see framework.md)
#   Module:   /{module}/{module}_intent.yaml (alongside implementation) - use {module}_intent.yaml, not bare intent.yaml
#   Task:     /{module}/intents/{task}_intent.yaml (intents/ subfolder required for level detection)
#
# EXAMPLES (Non-Code):
#   Book:     /book/book_system_intent.yaml (book root)
#   Chapter:  /book/manuscript/chapter-01/intent.yaml (alongside chapter.md)
#   Research: /research/analysis_intent.yaml (alongside findings.md)
#   Docs:     /docs/operations/runbook_intent.yaml (alongside runbook.md)
#   Guide:    /docs/onboarding/onboarding_guide_intent.yaml (alongside guide.md)
#
# REFERENCE: framework.md "Multi-Intent Naming Convention" section

# Optional: Reference a recipe if this follows a pattern
# recipe: "agent-classifier"
# recipe_version: "1.0"

# Parent intent: Establish hierarchy (recommended for all non-system intents)
# Uncomment and set the relative path to your parent intent:
# parent_intent: "../../system_intent.yaml"
#
# Hierarchy patterns:
#   Task → Module:   parent_intent: "../{module}_intent.yaml"
#   Module → Workflow: parent_intent: "../../workflows/{workflow}_intent.yaml"
#   Module → System: parent_intent: "../../system_intent.yaml"
#   Workflow → System: parent_intent: "../system_intent.yaml"

# SCOPE: What level is this intent for?
scope:
  level: "module"  # "system" | "workflow" | "module" | "task"
  type: "feature"  # "product" | "process" | "feature" | "agent" | "function" | "class" | "api" | "service" | "documentation" | "research" | "book"
  granularity: "coarse"  # "coarse" (system/workflow/module) | "fine" (task/function)

# ------------------------------------------------------------------------

e}_intent.yaml"
#   Module → Workflow: parent_intent: "../../workflows/{workflow}_intent.yaml"
#   Module → System: parent_intent: "../../system_intent.yaml"
#   Workflow → System: parent_intent: "../system_intent.yaml"

# SCOPE: What level is this intent for?
scope:
  level: "module"  # "system" | "workflow" | "module" | "task"
  type: "feature"  # "product" | "process" | "feature" | "agent" | "function" | "class" | "api" | "service" | "documentation" | "research" | "book"
  granularity: "coarse"  # "coarse" (system/workflow/module) | "fine" (task/function)

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# METADATA: Optional - for feature inventory and duplicate prevention (large projects)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Enables derived feature list: "what exists?" without a separate inventory file.
# Recommended when project has many modules or multiple teams.
# metadata:
#   feature_id: "csv_export"           # Stable, unique slug (lowercase, snake_case)
#   category: "data_export"            # Group for filtering (e.g. auth, reporting, api)
#   tags: ["admin", "compliance"]     # Searchable keywords
#   status: "implemented"             # implemented | planned | deprecated
#   introduced_version: "1.2.0"       # Optional: when added
#   deprecated_version: null          # Optional: when deprecated (if status: deprecated)

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# INVERSION OPPORTUNITIES (Optional - Principle 8: Innovation through Inversion)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Document default assumptions and proposed inversions considered when designing
# this feature. Use ivd_propose_inversions to brainstorm; then capture chosen/rejected here.
# inversion_opportunities:
#   problem: "Describe the specific problem this intent solves (e.g., CSV processing)"
#   dominant_belief: "The conventional or 'obvious' way to solve this problem."
#   proposed_inversions:
#     - name: "Inversion 1 (e.g., Don't load file into memory)"
#       description: "The opposite approach being considered."
#       rationale: "Why this inversion is promising (e.g., performance for large files)."
#       status: "chosen"   # chosen | rejected | deferred
#     - name: "Inversion 2 (e.g., Don't parse on server)"
#       description: "Another inverted approach."
#       rationale: "Why it was considered/rejected (e.g., security concerns)."
#       status: "rejected"

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# INTERFACE (Optional - MODULE-LEVEL: agents, MCP servers, APIs, CLIs, services)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Document the callable tool surface so consumers (AI agents, other modules,
# developers) know what this module exposes without reading implementation code.
#
# When to use:
# - scope.type is "agent", "service", "api", or module exposes tools/commands
# - MCP servers, AI agents with callable tools, REST/GraphQL APIs, CLIs
#
# Skip this section when:
# - Internal module with no external interface
# - Interf

---------------------------------------------------------------------
# INTERFACE (Optional - MODULE-LEVEL: agents, MCP servers, APIs, CLIs, services)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Document the callable tool surface so consumers (AI agents, other modules,
# developers) know what this module exposes without reading implementation code.
#
# When to use:
# - scope.type is "agent", "service", "api", or module exposes tools/commands
# - MCP servers, AI agents with callable tools, REST/GraphQL APIs, CLIs
#
# Skip this section when:
# - Internal module with no external interface
# - Interface is a single function (use task-level intent instead)
# - Pure data processing or library consumed only via import

# interface:
#   type: "mcp"                          # "mcp" | "agent" | "api" | "cli" | "service"
#
#   tools:
#     - name: "tool_name"
#       description: "One-line: what this tool does"
#       parameters:
#         - name: "param_name"
#           type: "string"               # string | number | boolean | object | array
#           required: true
#           description: "What this parameter is for"
#       returns: "What the tool returns (type or description)"
#       test: "tests/test_tool.py::test_tool_name"

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# ROLES (Optional, Experimental - MODULE-LEVEL: AI agents with multiple modes)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Document the behavioral modes of an AI agent that switches roles depending on
# context (e.g., implementer, reviewer, architect, teacher).
#
# Complements interface: interface = WHAT the agent can do (tools);
# roles = HOW the agent behaves in different contexts (constraints, tool subsets).
#
# When to use:
# - scope.type is "agent" and agent has 2+ distinct behavioral modes
# - Each mode has different constraints or tool subsets
# - Coordinator needs to understand behavioral modes for routing
#
# Skip this section when:
# - Agent has a single mode (just use interface)
# - Behavioral differences are trivial (tone/prompt only, no constraint changes)
# - Each role is a separate agent (use coordinator recipe instead)

# roles:
#   default: "implementer"                # which role the agent starts in
#
#   switching:
#     mechanism: "user_directed"           # user_directed | context_inferred | explicit_command
#     description: "How the agent transitions between roles"
#
#   definitions:
#     - name: "implementer"
#       description: "Writes code, creates files, executes commands"
#       when: "User requests building, fixing, or creating"
#       constraints:
#         - "Must write/update intent before implementing"
#         - "Must verify constraints after implementation"
#       tools: ["all"]                     # subset of interface.tools, or "all"
#       verification: "tests/test_role_implementer.py"
#
#     - name: "reviewer"
#       description: "Reviews code for quality, alignment, issues"
#       when: "User requests review, audit, or check"
#       constraints:
#         - "Read-only: no file modifications"
#         - "Must cite spe

description: "Writes code, creates files, executes commands"
#       when: "User requests building, fixing, or creating"
#       constraints:
#         - "Must write/update intent before implementing"
#         - "Must verify constraints after implementation"
#       tools: ["all"]                     # subset of interface.tools, or "all"
#       verification: "tests/test_role_implementer.py"
#
#     - name: "reviewer"
#       description: "Reviews code for quality, alignment, issues"
#       when: "User requests review, audit, or check"
#       constraints:
#         - "Read-only: no file modifications"
#         - "Must cite specific file and line references"
#       tools: ["read", "search", "validate"]
#       verification: "tests/test_role_reviewer.py"

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# AUTHORSHIP (Optional, Experimental - WHO originates and controls this intent)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Declare who can create, modify, or extend this intent artifact and what
# oversight is required. Formalizes the trust boundary for intent creation.
#
# When to use:
# - AI agents that design workflows or create sub-task intents autonomously
# - Self-improving systems where AI may modify its own intent
# - When teams need clarity on what AI can change without asking
#
# Skip this section when:
# - Standard IVD workflow (human describes, AI writes) — the default
# - Intent is always human-originated with no AI modification authority

# authorship:
#   origin: "human_directed"              # human_directed | ai_proposed | ai_autonomous
#   human_oversight: "review_required"    # review_required | audit_trail | escalation_only
#
#   ai_authority:
#     can_create: false                   # can AI create new sub-intents?
#     can_modify:                         # sections AI can modify without approval
#       - "implementation"
#       - "verification"
#     requires_approval:                  # fields needing human sign-off
#       - "intent.goal"
#       - "intent.success_metric"
#       - "constraints"
#
#   escalation: "When AI confidence < threshold or change impacts other intents"

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# EVALUATION (Optional, Experimental - continuous improvement loop)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Formalize how output quality is measured, what can be adjusted to improve it,
# and when the improvement loop should stop or escalate.
#
# Extends verification from binary (pass/fail) to continuous (how good, how
# to improve). Evaluation NEVER relaxes constraints — improves within them.
#
# When to use:
# - Autonomous workflows that run repeatedly and should improve
# - Self-improving agents that adjust process based on quality feedback
# - Production systems with measurable quality targets
#
# Skip this section when:
# - One-shot implementations with no iteration
# - Standard verification (constraints + tests) is sufficient
# - No feedback mechanism to measure quality

# evaluation:
#   criteria:
#     - metric: "output_quality_score"
#       ta

stop or escalate.

# Extends verification from binary (pass/fail) to continuous (how good, how
# to improve). Evaluation NEVER relaxes constraints — improves within them.
#
# When to use:
# - Autonomous workflows that run repeatedly and should improve
# - Self-improving agents that adjust process based on quality feedback
# - Production systems with measurable quality targets
#
# Skip this section when:
# - One-shot implementations with no iteration
# - Standard verification (constraints + tests) is sufficient

#

#     max_iterations: 3

#     escalate_when: "Cannot meet criteria after max_iterations"

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# PROJECT CONTEXT (Optional - SYSTEM-LEVEL ONLY)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Use this section for system-level intents to capture project-wide context.
# Child intents (workflow/module/task) reference the parent system intent via parent_intent.
#
# This enables:
# - Reusing project conventions when creating new intents
# - "Map to the stars" navigation (key paths, routes, entrypoints)
# - Consistent architecture and code rules across all intents
#
# When to use:
# - System-level (root) intent for a project/product
# - When installing IVD in an existing project (use ivd_init to scaffold)
# - When project has established conventions, architecture, tools
#
# Skip this section for:
# - Workflow, module, or task-level intents (reference parent_intent instead)
# - Greenfield projects with no existing conventions

# WHAT should this do?
intent:
  summary: "One-line description"
  goal: "Detailed explanation of purpose"
  success_metric: "How to measure success"
  stakeholders: ["Who cares about this"]

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# WORKFLOW: Multi-step process (REQUIRED for workflow-level intents)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Use this section for workflow-level intents (end-to-end processes)
# Documents the sequence of operations across multiple modules/functions

workflow:
  summary: "High-level description of what this workflow accomplishes"
  trigger: "What initiates this workflow (API call, event, schedule, etc.)"
  
  steps:
    - step: 1
      name: "Step name (verb + noun)"
      file: "path/to/file.py"
      function: "function_name()" 
      class: "ClassName"  # Optional if part of class
      description: "What this step does a

REQUIRED for workflow-level intents)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Use this section for workflow-level intents (end-to-end processes)
# Documents the sequence of operations across multiple modules/functions

workflow:
  summary: "High-level description of what this workflow accomplishes"
  trigger: "What initiates this workflow (API call, event, schedule, etc.)"
  
  steps:
    - step: 1
      name: "Step name (verb + noun)"
      file: "path/to/file.py"
      function: "function_name()" 
      class: "ClassName"  # Optional if part of class
      description: "What this step does and why"
      input: "What data comes into this step"
      output: "What data comes out of this step"
      business_logic: "Key business rules or decisions"
      performance: "p95 < 100ms"  # Optional performance target
      
    - step: 2
      name: "Next step"
      file: "path/to/another_file.py"
      function: "another_function()"
      description: "..."
      input: "Output from step 1"
      output: "..."
      business_logic: "..."
      depends_on: [1]  # Optional: explicit dependencies
  
  data_flow:
    - "High-level description of data transformations"
    - "Input → Step 1 → Step 2 → ... → Output"
    - "What data shape changes happen"
  
  error_handling:
    - step: 1
      failure_mode: "What can go wrong"
      action: "What happens when it fails"
      fallback: "Alternative path or default behavior"
      
    - step: 2
      failure_mode: "..."
      action: "..."
      retry_strategy: "Exponential backoff, 3 attempts"
  
  orchestration:
    type: "sequential | parallel | mixed"
    coordinator: "path/to/coordinator.py"
    description: |
      How steps are coordinated:
      - Sequential: Steps run one after another
      - Parallel: Steps run simultaneously
      - Mixed: Some sequential, some parallel
  
  performance:
    end_to_end_latency: "p95 < 5 seconds"
    throughput: ">= 100 workflows/minute"
    bottleneck_steps: ["step 3", "step 5"]
  
  monitoring:
    - metric: "workflow_completion_rate"
      target: ">= 95%"
      dashboard: "path/to/dashboard"
    - metric: "workflow_duration"
      target: "p95 < 5s"
      alert_threshold: "p95 > 10s"

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# FIELD MAPPING: Data sources, targets, and transformations (OPTIONAL)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Use this section for workflow-level or module-level intents that involve
# data movement, integration, or transformation between systems.
#
# When to use:
# - API integrations (CRM, payment, external services)
# - ETL pipelines and data synchronization
# - Database to database transfers
# - Data import/export features
# - Reporting and analytics data feeds
#
# Skip this section when:
# - No external data sources (internal-only logic)
# - Trivial 1:1 passthrough mapping
# - No data quality or compliance requirements
#
# See: recipes/data-field-mapping.yaml for complete pattern and examples

#   
#   sources:
#     - system:

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# INTERFACE: What goes in, what comes out? (REQUIRED for task-level intents)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Use this section for task-level intents (functions, API endpoints, agents)
# Skip for module-level or workflow-level intents (unless clear I/O)

interface:
  signature: "function_name(param1: Type, param2: Type) -> ReturnType"
  
  inputs:
    - name: "parameter_name"
      type: "str | int | dict | list | CustomType"
      description: "What this input represents and its purpose"
      req

r alert"

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# INTERFACE: What goes in, what comes out? (REQUIRED for task-level intents)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Use this section for task-level intents (functions, API endpoints, agents)
# Skip for module-level or workflow-level intents (unless clear I/O)

interface:
  signature: "function_name(param1: Type, param2: Type) -> ReturnType"
  
  inputs:
    - name: "parameter_name"
      type: "str | int | dict | list | CustomType"
      description: "What this input represents and its purpose"
      required: true  # true | false
      default: "default_value"  # if not required
      constraints:
        - "length >= 1"
        - "format: email"
        - "range: 0-100"
      example: "example_value"
      validation: "How input is validated"
      
    - name: "optional_parameter"
      type: "dict"
      description: "Optional configuration"
      required: false
      default: "{}"
      example: {"key": "value"}
  
  outputs:
    - name: "result"
      type: "dict | list | bool | CustomType"
      description: "What gets returned and what it represents"
      structure: |
        {
          "status": "success|error",
          "data": {...},
          "metadata": {...}
        }
      constraints:
        - "status must be 'success' or 'error'"
        - "data present when status=success"
      example:
        status: "success"
        data: {"score": 0.85}
        metadata: {"timestamp": "2026-01-23T10:00:00Z"}
      
    - name: "side_effects"
      type: "effects"
      description: "What else changes (database writes, API calls, etc.)"
      effects:
        - "Updates database table: leads"
        - "Sends event to: analytics_queue"
        - "Logs to: CloudWatch"
  
  exceptions:
    - exception: "ValueError"
      when: "Input validation fails"
      handling: "Raise with descriptive message"
      
    - exception: "APIError"
      when: "External service unavailable"
      handling: "Retry with exponential backoff (3 attempts)"
  
  dependencies:
    - dependency: "external_service"
      type: "API"
      required: true
      fallback: "Cache or default value"
      
    - dependency: "database_connection"
      type: "database"
      required: true
      fallback: "None (fail fast)"

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# BEHAVIOR: How should this behave? (REQUIRED for task-level intents)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Use this section for task-level intents (functions, methods)
# Skip for workflow-level or module-level intents

behavior:
  preconditions:
    - "Input must be non-empty"
    - "User must be authenticated"
    - "Rate limit not exceeded"
  
  postconditions:
    - "Result is always returned (success or error)"
    - "Side effects are atomic (all or nothing)"
    - "Logs capture full execution trace"
  
  invariants:
    - "Function is idempotent (same input = same output)"
    - "No global state modification"
    - "Thread-safe execution"
  
  performance:
    - metric: "latency"
      tar

-----------------------------------------
# Use this section for task-level intents (functions, methods)
# Skip for workflow-level or module-level intents

behavior:
  preconditions:
    - "Input must be non-empty"
    - "User must be authenticated"
    - "Rate limit not exceeded"
  
  postconditions:
    - "Result is always returned (success or error)"
    - "Side effects are atomic (all or nothing)"
    - "Logs capture full execution trace"
  
  invariants:
    - "Function is idempotent (same input = same output)"
    - "No global state modification"
    - "Thread-safe execution"
  
  performance:
    - metric: "latency"
      target: "p95 < 200ms"
      critical_threshold: "p95 > 500ms"
      
    - metric: "throughput"
      target: ">= 100 requests/second"
      critical_threshold: "< 50 requests/second"
      
    - metric: "memory"
      target: "< 100MB per request"
      critical_threshold: "> 500MB per request"

# WHAT must hold true?
constraints:
  - name: "constraint_id"
    requirement: "metric >= threshold"
    test: "path/to/test.py::test_name"
    consequence_if_violated: "What breaks if this fails"

# WHY this approach?
rationale:
  decision: "Key decision made"
  reasoning: |
    Explain the reasoning behind design choices:
    - Why this algorithm/approach?
    - Why these input/output structures?
    - Why these performance targets?
    - What trade-offs were accepted?
  evidence: "Link to validation (notebook, doc, etc.)"
  date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
  stakeholder: "Who approved this"

# WHY NOT alternatives?
alternatives:
  - name: "alternative_approach"
    rejected_because: "Reason"
    experiment: "path/to/comparison"
    date_tested: "YYYY-MM-DD"

# WHAT could go wrong?
risks:
  - condition: "When does this become a problem?"
    action: "What should happen?"
    monitor: "Where to watch for this"
    severity: "high|medium|low"

# WHERE is this implemented?
implementation:
  current: "path/to/file.py"
  function: "function_name"  # For task-level intents
  class: "ClassName"  # If part of a class
  version: 1
  tests: "path/to/tests/"
  documentation: "path/to/docs.md"

# HOW to verify this works? (Test cases for task-level intents)
verification:
  test_cases:
    - name: "happy_path"
      description: "Normal successful execution"
      input:
        param1: "valid_value"
        param2: 123
      expected_output:
        status: "success"
        data: {"result": "expected"}
      validates_constraint: "output_structure"
      
    - name: "edge_case_empty_input"
      description: "Handle empty input gracefully"
      input:
        param1: ""
        param2: 0
      expected_output:
        status: "error"
        error: "Input validation failed"
      validates_constraint: "input_validation"
      
    - name: "error_handling"
      description: "External service failure"
      input:
        param1: "valid"
      mock: "external_service returns 503"
      expected_output:
        status: "error"
        error: "Service unavailable"
      validates_constraint: "resilience"
  
  property_tests:
    - property: "idempotency"
      test: "Same input always produces same output"
      verification: "tests/test_properties

pty input gracefully"
      input:
        param1: ""
        param2: 0
      expected_output:
        status: "error"
        error: "Input validation failed"
      validates_constraint: "input_validation"
      
    - name: "error_handling"
      description: "External service failure"
      input:
        param1: "valid"
      mock: "external_service returns 503"
      expected_output:
        status: "error"
        error: "Service unavailable"
      validates_constraint: "resilience"
  
  property_tests:
    - property: "idempotency"
      test: "Same input always produces same output"
      verification: "tests/test_properties.py::test_idempotent"
      
    - property: "type_safety"
      test: "Invalid types raise TypeError"
      verification: "tests/test_types.py::test_type_validation"

# WHEN was this changed?
changelog:
  - version: 1
    date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
    change: "Initial implementation"
    reason: "Why this was created"
    evidence: "Link to validation if any"
